body:
### Describe your issue

When `restrict` type qualifier helps the compiler to vectorise to loop [1]. It is can be useful for projects using Cython. E.g. I was able to rewrite scikit-image _extrema_cy.pyx to use `restrict` keyword (using patched Cython)


Original version:
```cython
cdef inline void _mark_candidates_in_last_dimension(
        dtype_t[::1] image, unsigned char[::1] flags) noexcept nogil:
    cdef Py_ssize_t i, i_ahead
    i = 1
    while i < image.shape[0]:
        if image[i - 1] < image[i] and flags[i] != BORDER_INDEX:
            # Potential maximum (in last dimension) is found, find
            # other edge of current plateau or "edge of dimension"
            i_ahead = i + 1
            while (
                image[i] == image[i_ahead] and
                flags[i_ahead] != BORDER_INDEX
            ):
                i_ahead += 1
            if image[i] > image[i_ahead]:
                # Found local maximum (in one dimension), mark all
                # parts of the plateau as potential maximum
                flags[i:i_ahead] = CANDIDATE
            i = i_ahead
        else:
            i += 1
```

version using `restrict` keyword for which I am dirty PoC:

```cython
cdef inline void _mark_candidates_in_last_dimension(
        dtype_t[::1] image, unsigned char[::1] flags) noexcept nogil:
    cdef Py_ssize_t i, i_ahead
    # Cast data pointers to C pointers with restrict
    cdef restrict dtype_t* im_ptr = <dtype_t*> &image[0]
    cdef restrict unsigned char* fl_ptr = <unsigned char*> &flags[0]

    i = 1
    while i < image.shape[0]:
        if im_ptr[i - 1] < im_ptr[i] and fl_ptr[i] != BORDER_INDEX:
            i_ahead = i + 1
            while im_ptr[i] == im_ptr[i_ahead] and fl_ptr[i_ahead] != BORDER_INDEX:
                i_ahead += 1
            if im_ptr[i] > im_ptr[i_ahead]:
                # mark plateau as candidate
                for j in range(i, i_ahead):
                    fl_ptr[j] = CANDIDATE
            i = i_ahead
        else:
            i += 1
```

Ideal version I would like to have (but can be tricky to implement):

```cython
cdef inline void _mark_candidates_in_last_dimension(
        restrict dtype_t[::1] image, restrict unsigned char[::1] flags) noexcept nogil:
    cdef Py_ssize_t i, i_ahead

    i = 1
    while i < image.shape[0]:
        if image[i - 1] < image[i] and flags[i] != BORDER_INDEX:
            i_ahead = i + 1
            while image[i] == image[i_ahead] and flags[i_ahead] != BORDER_INDEX:
                i_ahead += 1
            if image[i] > image[i_ahead]:
                # mark plateau as candidate
                for j in range(i, i_ahead):
                    flags[j] = CANDIDATE
            i = i_ahead
        else:
            i += 1
```

New version of code gave me ~2% speedup in following test:

```python
In [1]: import skimage as ski
   ...: import numpy as np
   ...: img = np.random.randint(0, 255, (8000, 8000), dtype=np.uint8)
In [2]: %timeit ski.morphology.local_maxima(img)
```


* No restrict

```
519 ms ± 1.49 ms per loop (mean ± std. dev. of 7 runs, 1 loop each)
517 ms ± 731 μs per loop (mean ± std. dev. of 7 runs, 1 loop each)
525 ms ± 19 ms per loop (mean ± std. dev. of 7 runs, 1 loop each)
```

* restrict

```
506 ms ± 586 μs per loop (mean ± std. dev. of 7 runs, 1 loop each)
510 ms ± 5.56 ms per loop (mean ± std. dev. of 7 runs, 1 loop each)
509 ms ± 1.43 ms per loop (mean ± std. dev. of 7 runs, 1 loop each)
```

Downsides of the proposal:
* Using `restrict` can be dangerous. The memory behind `restrict` pointer must be accessed only via given pointer. Otherwise behaviour is undefined, most probably nasty crash.
* When `restrict` is used properly, the compiler uses SIMD instruction for parallel execution. This can lead to worse performance when the loop is looping over smaler dataset
* The syntax is cumbersome for memoryviews. It would be great to introduce syntax sugar `restrict <TYPE>[::1]` which will cause that underlying `.data` field is access via restrict pointer. But not sure how complex it would be to implement it.


The syntax is up to discussion. C language gives restrict keyworkd after `*` so there are more possibilities how to implement it.
```
cdef restrict TYPE *a = ...
cdef TYPE * restrict a = ...
```
The Cython implementation should be fairly limited, in my oppinion we should focus only on limited usages of `restrict` keyword (e.g. only in function parameters)


[1] https://en.cppreference.com/w/c/language/restrict.html
[2] https://github.com/scikit-image/scikit-image/blob/main/src/skimage/morphology/_extrema_cy.pyx

comment_data.body:
I think it's probably worthwhile.

One thing to be slightly careful of is that it isn't part of C++ so is really valid for C code only. I don't think we need to enforce that.

-----------------------------------------------

> Otherwise behaviour is undefined, most probably nasty crash.

Most of the time it isn't actually that bad - it just lets the compiler skip reads and assume that data is unchanged. Obviously "undefined" means "anything" but in practice it's usually not a crash.

------------------------------------------------

Memoryviews are sort of interesting. One of the things that Rust claims to give you is that if you have a mutable reference to an object then you know that no-where else is using any kind of reference to it. And if you have an immutable reference then you know that no-where else can be changing it. That gives the optimization advantages of strict in a slightly better defined way.

The Python buffer protocol doesn't let you express that behaviour, and some Rust users have been keen to get it added (although haven't yet).

The current proposed approach is just "take it on trust that users are doing the right thing with memoryviews". But we possibly should be prepared to enforce it properly in future if it becomes part of the buffer protocol.

> One thing to be slightly careful of is that it isn't part of C++ so is really valid for C code only.

As long as I understand it is supported via compiler specific constructs: [GCC `__restrict__`](https://gcc.gnu.org/onlinedocs/gcc/Restricted-Pointers.html), [MSVC `__restrict`](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/cpp/cpp/extension-restrict?view=msvc-170) and [CLANG `__restrict__`](https://clang.llvm.org/docs/LanguageExtensions.html#const-const-volatile-volatile-restrict-restrict)

title: [ENH] Introduce `restrict` qualifier
